description: A conceptual diagram illustrating the calculation of the Signal-to-Clutter Ratio (SCR) using amplitude data from a grid-based spatial or spatio-temporal stack of images. The diagram displays a 6x6 grid, stacked six layers deep, which can represent multiple observations over time or different data channels, relevant to methodologies like Interferometric Synthetic Aperture Radar (InSAR). A central pixel, labelled `Pc` (dark green), represents the 'signal' point. Surrounding `Pc` in a cross pattern are eight pixels (light green) which represent 'clutter' or 'noise' points, generically labelled `Pi`. Other pixels in the grid are shown in orange. The associated formula defines SCR as: `SCR = A^2(Pc) / (1/NP * Σ(i∈Red) A^2(Pi))`. In this formula, `A^2(Pc)` is the squared amplitude of the central pixel `Pc`. The denominator is the average of the squared amplitudes of neighboring pixels, where `NP` is the number of pixels in the set `i∈Red` (corresponding to the eight light green clutter pixels), and `Σ(i∈Red) A^2(Pi)` is the sum of the squared amplitudes of these `NP` clutter pixels. This model is used in amplitude-based algorithms for selecting Multi-Temporal Persistent Scatterers (MPS) by comparing a central pixel's amplitude to its neighbours.
